label: Asymmetric Stack
description: Vertical, scroll-rhythm landing page that abandons the split-hero entirely. Uses oversized typography blocks anchored to alternating sides of the viewport, floating illustrations or product fragments that overlap section boundaries, and a tall column rhythm — for design studios, creative agencies, indie products, and anything that wants to feel made by hand rather than templated. The hero is a tall typography block, not a 60/40 grid.
signatureMotif: alternating left/right anchored typography blocks with floating illustration fragments that bleed across section boundaries — vertical rhythm replaces hero-split
promptHints: Build the hero as a tall vertical typography block, NOT a left-text/right-image grid. Anchor the headline hard to one side (left or right), let the opposite side breathe with empty space, and place a small illustration or product fragment floating asymmetrically across the boundary into the next section.

Use the whole page as a scroll-rhythm composition: each section anchors typography to the opposite side from the section above (left → right → left → right). This creates a zig-zag reading path that feels designed by hand.

Floating elements (small illustrations, photo fragments, sticky badges) should overlap section boundaries — bottom of section #1 contains the top half of an element whose bottom half sits inside section #2. This visually stitches the page together vertically.

layouts: Hero is a tall section (min-h-[80vh] or similar) with a single oversized headline anchored hard to the left edge (max-w-2xl, no centering), a short standfirst directly below, and one CTA — the right half of the viewport stays empty except for one floating illustration fragment placed off-grid.

Section #2 anchors its content to the RIGHT (mirroring the hero), with the headline and supporting copy in a max-w-xl right-aligned column, and the left half empty except for a number, a thin rule, or a small mark — alternating rhythm between sections.

Use a vertically stacked feature list (not a grid): each feature gets its own min-h-[40vh] block with one large numeral (01, 02, 03...) on the alternating side, a short heading, and a single sentence — no card surfaces, no horizontal grids.

Place floating illustrations or product fragments so they OVERLAP section boundaries: the bottom 40% of section #1 contains the top 40% of an element whose remaining 60% extends into section #2 (use absolute positioning + negative top/bottom margins).
Footer/final-CTA section anchors hard to one side again with a single oversized question or invitation, no card, no form-block — just type, the CTA, and generous bottom padding (py-32+).
motifs: Typography is the primary surface: headlines use a serif or expressive display face at clamp(3rem, 8vw, 6rem) with leading 1.0 and tracking tight; body copy stays at 17-19px in a calm sans, generous line-height (1.65+).
Floating fragments are illustrations, photo crops, or small product details (NOT full screenshots) that read as collage — picture them as torn paper or magazine cutouts placed deliberately off-grid.
Background is mostly off-white (#FAFAF8 or similar warm paper tone) or a single quiet color; no gradient meshes, no glow spills. The composition does the work, not the surface.
Use thin rules (1px), small-cap eyebrow labels, and large numerals as the only repeating UI elements — no cards, no shadows, no chips, no badges.
antiPatterns: Never use a left-text + right-image grid for the hero. The whole point of this variant is breaking that template — if the hero looks like a 60/40 split, the variant has failed.
Avoid card-based feature grids (3-up or 4-up cards with icons). Replace with tall stacked feature blocks anchored to alternating sides.
Do not use heavy product screenshots or dashboard mockups; this variant favors illustration fragments, photographic crops, or no imagery at all over saturated UI screenshots.
Avoid centered everything (centered hero text, centered CTAs, centered cards) — centering kills the asymmetric rhythm. Anchor each block hard to one edge.
Never stack content as one continuous narrow centered column (max-w-3xl mx-auto for the entire page) — this variant requires the alternating left/right rhythm to read.
